- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- lightfast-changelog: Write and review AEO-optimized changelog entries for Lightfast. Triggers when the user asks
to write a changelog entry, review changelog content, or asks about changelog structure,
AEO optimization, or release notes formatting.
- lightfast-sdk: Use Lightfast tools to search across team knowledge and interact with connected providers
(GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, Apollo). Triggers when the user asks about errors, issues,
PRs, deployments, or wants to search across their dev tools. Also triggers proactively when
investigating bugs, reviewing code, or checking deployment status.
- lightfast-inngest: Inspect Inngest function runs, debug failures, send events, and invoke functions via the
local Inngest dev server MCP. Triggers when the user asks about background jobs, workflow
runs, failed steps, event delivery, or wants to invoke/test Inngest functions.
- lightfast-db: Query and inspect the Postgres database schema and data. Triggers when the user asks about
tables, columns, rows, database schema, wants to run a SELECT query, or needs to understand
the data model. Read-only — cannot modify data.
- turborepo: Turborepo monorepo build system guidance. Triggers on: turbo.json, task pipelines,
dependsOn, caching, remote cache, the "turbo" CLI, --filter, --affected, CI optimization, environment
variables, internal packages, monorepo structure/best practices, and boundaries.

Use when user: configures tasks/workflows/pipelines, creates packages, sets up
monorepo, shares code between apps, runs changed/affected packages, debugs cache,
or has apps/packages directories.
- implement_plan: Implement technical plans from thoughts/shared/plans with verification
- create_plan: Create detailed implementation plans through interactive research and iteration
- validate_plan: Validate implementation against plan, verify success criteria, identify issues
- commit: Create git commits with user approval and no Claude attribution
- oneshot_merge: Commit changes, create PR, and merge into main in one shot
- improve_plan: Adversarial review of implementation plans — find weaknesses, kill code smells, improve DX
- research_codebase: Document codebase as-is with thoughts directory for historical context
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

27	- BreadcrumbList (Home > Changelog > entry)

37	- Question-based headings targeting query fanout
38	- Self-contained sections for chunk-level retrieval
39	- FAQ items targeting conversational sub-queries
40	- Trust signals (author credentials, TL;DR summary)
41	
42	## File Naming
43	
44	```
45	apps/www/src/content/changelog/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.mdx
46	```
47	
48	Use the release date. The slug becomes the URL path: `/changelog/YYYY-MM-DD-slug`.
49	
50	## Frontmatter Schema
51	
52	All fields are required unless marked optional.
53	
54	```yaml
55	---
56	title: "Short, descriptive title"                    # min 1 char
57	description: "50-160 char summary for meta tags"     # min 50, max 160
58	keywords: ["keyword1", "keyword2", ...]              # 3-20 items
59	canonicalUrl: "https://lightfast.ai/changelog/..."   # optional, must start with https://lightfast.ai/changelog/
60	ogTitle: "Title for social sharing"                  # min 1, max 70 chars
61	ogDescription: "50-160 char OG description"          # min 50, max 160
62	noindex: false
63	nofollow: false
64	authors:
65	  - name: "Author Name"
66	    url: "https://lightfast.ai"
67	    twitterHandle: "@handle"
68	    jobTitle: "Role"                                  # optional, emitted in Person JSON-LD
69	publishedAt: "YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ"                 # ISO datetime
70	updatedAt: "YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ"                   # ISO datetime
71	version: "v0.X.0"                                    # min 1 char
72	type: "feature"                                      # feature | improvement | fix | breaking
73	featuredImage: "/images/changelog/..."               # optional, must start with /images/
74	tldr: "20-300 char summary"                          # acts as trust block answer summary
75	faq:                                                 # min 1 item
76	  - question: "Question text"                        # min 10 chars
77	    answer: "Answer text"                            # min 20 chars
78	---
79	```
80	
81	## Writing the Content Body
82	
83	### 1. Opening Paragraph (Answer-First Summary)
84	
85	Lead with a 30-60 word direct answer to "What's in this release?" This is the first thing both humans and AI engines see after the TL;DR box.
86	
87	```markdown
88	Lightfast vX.Y.Z introduces [primary change], [secondary change], and [tertiary change].
89	```
90	
91	Do NOT use filler like "Here's everything in vX.Y.Z." — that wastes the most valuable content position.
92	
93	### 2. Headings (Question-Based, Targeting Query Fanout)
94	
95	Every H2 heading must be phrased as a question that mirrors how users ask AI engines.
96	
97	| Bad (declarative) | Good (question-based) |
98	|---|---|
99	| `## Neural Pipeline` | `## How does the neural pipeline process events?` |
100	| `## New Features` | `## What new features does vX.Y.Z add?` |
101	| `## Breaking Changes` | `## What breaking changes are in vX.Y.Z?` |
102	| `## Bug Fixes` | `## What bugs were fixed in vX.Y.Z?` |
103	| `## API` | `## How do I access Lightfast programmatically?` |
104	| `## Performance` | `## What performance improvements ship in vX.Y.Z?` |
105	
106	**Why:** AI engines fan out a user's prompt into 3-10 sub-queries before retrieving content. A heading like "How does the neural pipeline process events?" directly matches the sub-query an engine generates from "How does Lightfast work?"
107	
108	### 3. Section Body (Atomic, Self-Contained)
109	
110	Each H2 section follows this structure:
111	
112	1. **Direct answer** (30-60 words) — answers the heading question immediately
113	2. **Atomic paragraphs** (2-3 paragraphs, 1-3 sentences each) — context and detail
114	3. **Scannable bullet list** — key points at a glance
115	4. **Example or screenshot** (when applicable)
116	
117	Each section MUST be self-contained. An AI engine should be able to extract any H2 section without needing context from other sections or the page intro.
118	
119	```markdown
120	## How does Lightfast track entities across sources?
121	
122	The Entities view is a real-time browser for every entity extracted
123	from your engineering events — repositories, pull requests,
124	deployments, issues, users, endpoints, and more.
125	
126	Filter by category, search by name, and watch new entities appear
127	live as events are processed. Each entity links to a detail view
128	showing its full metadata and event timeline.
129	
130	- Real-time streaming via server-sent events
131	- Filter by entity category
132	- Search by entity name
133	- Detail views with full metadata and event timeline
134	```
135	
136	### 4. Atomic Paragraphs
137	
138	- 1-3 sentences per paragraph
139	- Each paragraph conveys one idea
140	- No long-form prose blocks
141	- No paragraphs that depend on the previous paragraph for context
142	
143	### 5. Feature-Specific Sections
144	
145	For each major feature, create a dedicated H2 section with its own question-based heading. Do NOT combine multiple features under a single "New Features" heading — each feature is a separate retrievable chunk.
146	
147	### 6. Improvements / Minor Changes
148	
149	Group minor changes under a single H2 like `## What other improvements ship in vX.Y.Z?` with a bullet list. Each bullet should be a complete, self-contained statement.
150	
151	```markdown
152	## What other improvements ship in v0.2.0?
153	
154	Beyond the core features, v0.2.0 includes:
155	
156	- **Real-time updates** — entities and events stream live via SSE across all views
157	- **Jobs dashboard** — monitor background workflows with live status and error details
158	```
159	
160	## Writing FAQ Items
161	
162	FAQ items in the frontmatter become FAQPage JSON-LD — the single most impactful structured data type for AEO (per Profound's ML model, FAQPage schema is preferred over generic Article schema).
163	
164	### Guidelines
165	
166	- **5-7 FAQ items per entry** — cover the major features and common questions
167	- **Target query fanout sub-queries** — what would an AI engine ask when decomposing a user's prompt?
168	- **Direct, concise answers** — 1-3 sentences, no long explanatory text
169	- **Self-contained** — each answer must make sense without reading other FAQ items
170	- **Include product name** — "Lightfast" should appear in answers for entity recognition
171	
172	### FAQ Categories to Cover
173	
174	1. **Discovery** — "What is [feature]?" or "What does [feature] do?"
175	2. **Mechanism** — "How does [feature] work?"
176	3. **Capability** — "Can I [do something]?" or "Does Lightfast support [X]?"
177	4. **Specifics** — "What [entity types / integrations / etc.] does Lightfast [verb]?"
178	
179	### Example
180	
181	```yaml
182	faq:
183	  - question: "What is Lightfast and what does it do?"
184	    answer: "Lightfast is an engineering intelligence platform. It connects to your developer tools, processes events through a neural pipeline that extracts entities and relationships, and lets you query everything with AI."
185	  - question: "What sources does Lightfast connect to in v0.1.0?"
186	    answer: "Lightfast connects to GitHub, Vercel, Linear, and Sentry via OAuth. Events flow in real-time through webhooks and are automatically processed by the neural pipeline."
187	  - question: "How does Lightfast's AI chat work?"
188	    answer: "Explore is a streaming AI interface that searches your entity graph, retrieves context from the vector store, and responds with cited, markdown-formatted answers grounded in your actual engineering data."
189	```
190	
191	## Freshness
192	
193	- Always set `updatedAt` to the current date when editing an existing entry
194	- If a feature described in an old entry has changed significantly, update the entry content AND the `updatedAt` date
195	- Recency is an explicit ranking signal in AI engine content scoring
196	
197	## Images and Media
198	
199	- Featured images go in `apps/www/public/images/changelog/`
200	- Use `{/* TODO: description — path */}` comments for planned screenshots
201	- The `![alt text](/images/changelog/filename.png)` syntax renders via the MDX `img` component as a Next.js Image with `aspect-video` styling
202	- OG images are generated programmatically at `[slug]/opengraph-image.tsx` — no manual OG image needed
203

205	
206	The changelog index page at `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(marketing)/(content)/changelog/page.tsx` has its own hardcoded FAQ array. When adding new changelog entries that introduce new product capabilities, consider whether the index-page FAQ should be updated to reflect the broader product.
207	
208	## Key Files
209	
210	| Purpose | Path |
211	|---|---|
212	| MDX content files | `apps/www/src/content/changelog/*.mdx` |
213	| Frontmatter schema (Zod) | `apps/www/src/lib/content-schemas.ts` |
214	| Content loader | `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(content)/_lib/source.ts` |
215	| SEO pipeline | `apps/www/src/lib/seo-bundle.ts` |
216	| JSON-LD builder | `apps/www/src/lib/builders/changelog.ts` |
217	| Shared builders | `apps/www/src/lib/builders/shared.ts` |
218	| Index page | `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(marketing)/(content)/changelog/page.tsx` |
219	| Entry page | `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(marketing)/(content)/changelog/[slug]/page.tsx` |
220	| OG image | `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(marketing)/(content)/changelog/[slug]/opengraph-image.tsx` |
221	| Feed generator | `apps/www/src/app/(app)/_lib/feeds/generate-changelog-feed.ts` |
222	| MDX components | `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(content)/_lib/mdx-components.tsx` |
223	| Sitemap | `apps/www/src/app/sitemap.ts` |
224	
225	## Checklist
226	
227	Before finalizing a changelog entry, verify:
228	
229	- [ ] Opening paragraph is 30-60 words answering "What's in this release?"
230	- [ ] Every H2 is a question that mirrors a conversational query
231	- [ ] Every H2 section is self-contained (extractable without surrounding context)
232	- [ ] Every section leads with a direct answer before detail
233	- [ ] Paragraphs are atomic (1-3 sentences, one idea each)
234	- [ ] 5-7 FAQ items covering discovery, mechanism, capability, and specifics
235	- [ ] FAQ answers are concise (1-3 sentences) and self-contained

237	- [ ] `description` is 50-160 chars
238	- [ ] `ogTitle` is max 70 chars
239	- [ ] `keywords` has 3-20 items
240	- [ ] `updatedAt` is set to today if editing an existing entry
241	- [ ] `authors` includes `jobTitle` for trust signals
242	- [ ] Product name "Lightfast" appears in FAQ answers and section openings

| `## Breaking Changes` | `## What breaking changes are in vX.Y.Z?` |


Here's what I found and my recommendation.

**Repo reality:** there are 6 content types in `apps/www/src/content/` (api, blog, changelog, docs, integrations, legal). All extend shared bases in `content-schemas.ts`, all have parallel builders in `lib/builders/`. The AEO methodology you wrote applies equally to every one of them.

**The current skill conflates three layers:**
1. *Stable* — AEO methodology, query fanout, FAQ structure, atomic paragraphs (timeless)
2. *Volatile* — frontmatter fields, character limits, file paths (rots when schema changes)
3. *Content-type-specific* — changelog index page FAQ, OG image route (only true for changelog)

Layer 2 is the rot risk you flagged. Layer 3 makes the skill feel narrow.

**Three options:**

**A. Restructure in place** — keep `lightfast-changelog`, strip schema details, replace with "read `content-schemas.ts` for current frontmatter, run typecheck to validate." Smallest change. Still single-purpose.

**B. Split into two** — `lightfast-aeo` (pure methodology, no Lightfast specifics) + `lightfast-content` (thin orchestrator that discovers schema/paths per content type). Cleanest separation. Two surfaces to maintain.

**C. One general `lightfast-content` skill** — replaces `lightfast-changelog`, covers all 6 types. Workflow = pick type → read its schema + builder → apply AEO playbook → typecheck. Mirrors the repo. Methodology lives once.

I'd go with **C**. The methodology is the durable asset; the per-type details should be discovered, not memorized. Skill body becomes: *Principles* (stable AEO knowledge) → *Discovery* (here's where current schema/paths live, go read them) → *Workflow* → *Checklist* (methodology-only, no character counts — typecheck owns constraints).

Want me to sketch the file layout for C before writing?

input.prompt:
I'm designing a durable "AEO methodology" skill for an AI coding assistant — it'll be the root best-practices reference that per-content-type skills (changelog, blog, docs) sit on top of. Profound (tryprofound.com) is the most-cited source for Answer Engine Optimization methodology because they have ML models trained on millions of top-cited pages across AI platforms (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Google AI Overviews, etc.). I want to ground the skill in what they actually say rather than what I half-remember.

## Task

Comprehensively scrape and synthesize tryprofound.com's public editorial content — primarily the blog, but also include any /research, /resources, /guides, /learn, /glossary, or similar sections that contain AEO methodology. Start by crawling the site to enumerate what exists, then pull down each relevant article.

## What I care about

The output should be a structured knowledge synthesis I can use to draft a skill. Focus on *durable editorial principles*, not product pitches or case studies. Specifically, extract:

1. **Retrieval model** — how AI engines chunk, retrieve, and cite content. What's the unit of retrieval?
2. **Query fanout** — how user prompts decompose into sub-queries, and what that means for headings.
3. **Structural patterns** — heading styles, paragraph shape, section self-containment, answer-first ordering.
4. **Schema/structured data** — which JSON-LD types matter most and why (FAQPage vs. Article vs. BlogPosting, etc.).
5. **FAQ construction** — how to write Q&A pairs that get cited. Count, length, topic coverage patterns.
6. **Trust & authority signals** — author markup, jobTitle, citations, freshness, E-E-A-T analogs for AEO.
7. **Entity & brand mentions** — product name repetition, entity recognition patterns.
8. **Content formats that win citations** — lists, tables, definitions, comparisons, how-tos.
9. **Anti-patterns** — what they explicitly say NOT to do.
10. **Measurement** — how they quantify AEO performance (if mentioned).

Also note any principles that appear across multiple articles — convergence is a strong signal.

## Output format

Return a single synthesis document organized by the categories above. For each principle:
- State the principle in one sentence
- Quote or paraphrase the supporting Profound claim
- Cite the source URL
- Flag confidence: **Strong** (explicit claim, multiple articles) / **Medium** (one article, explicit) / **Inferred** (you connected dots)

End with a short section listing anything Profound *doesn't* cover that I'd still need for a well-rounded AEO skill.

## Constraints

- Skip marketing pages, case studies, and pricing pages unless they contain methodology.
- If an article is shallow/promotional, note the URL and skip.
- Don't invent principles that aren't in the source. If you're unsure, mark it Inferred or leave it out.
- Aim for breadth over depth — I'd rather have 30 well-sourced principles than 10 with long commentary.
- No need to verify against source code (this isn't a library API); the web-analyzer "verify against source" step doesn't apply here. Confidence comes from *whether Profound said it* and *how often*.

content.text:
I now have comprehensive source material across all ten categories. Here is the full synthesis document.

---

## Analysis: Profound (tryprofound.com) AEO Methodology — Comprehensive Knowledge Synthesis

### Summary

Profound has published the most data-grounded public AEO methodology available, drawing on datasets ranging from 80,000 to 700,000+ real prompts across ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and other engines. Their core thesis is that AI engines retrieve at the chunk level, fan user prompts out into multiple sub-queries, and cite a handful of sources per response from a probabilistic and highly volatile pool. Content that wins citations is structurally explicit, answer-first, schema-marked, and distributed to sources AI engines already trust — with platform-specific strategies required because engine citation pools overlap by only 6–16%.

---

### Key Findings by Category

---

#### 1. Retrieval Model — How AI Engines Chunk, Retrieve, and Cite Content

**Finding 1.1 — Chunk-level retrieval, not page-level**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: AI search engines do not index or retrieve whole pages. They break content into passages and retrieve the most relevant segments for synthesis.
- **Quote**: "AI search engines don't index or retrieve whole pages — they break content into passages or 'chunks' and retrieve the most relevant segments for synthesis. That's why you should optimize each section like a standalone snippet." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/aeo-vs-geo (citing Aleyda Solis checklist, endorsed by Profound)
- **Provenance**: AEO vs GEO article (June 2025), repeated as foundational principle in AEO content score article (Oct 2025) and GEO framework (July 2025)

**Finding 1.2 — RAG is the underlying retrieval architecture**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: AI engines use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) — real-time web retrieval to augment model responses — prioritizing relevance, recency, and trust.
- **Quote**: "AI engines rely on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), the process of augmenting a generative model with external documents retrieved in real time to produce more accurate answers. This retrieval logic prioritizes relevance, recency, and trust. This means that ChatGPT now does web search for most questions instead of relying on its existing training data." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/generative-engine-optimization-geo-guide-2025
- **Provenance**: GEO Framework (July 2025)

**Finding 1.3 — Turn 1 is the critical citation moment**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed from ~700K conversation sample)
- **Claim**: Turn 1 of a conversation is 2.5x more likely to trigger citations than Turn 10, and nearly 4x more likely than Turn 20. Opening questions trigger web searches; follow-ups rarely do.
- **Quote**: "Turn 1 is everything. Users' opening questions trigger web searches; follow-ups rarely do. Turn 1 is 2.5× more likely to trigger citations than turn 10, and nearly 4× more likely than turn 20. If you want to be cited, you need to win the 'first question.'" — https://tryprofound.com/blog/chatgpt-citation-sources
- **Provenance**: Original research on ~730,000 ChatGPT conversations, Oct–Dec 2025

**Finding 1.4 — AI cites ~4–6 sources per turn, not one winner**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed)
- **Claim**: When ChatGPT cites, it cites ~4 unique domains per turn and ~6 per conversation. 66% of cited turns have 1–4 unique sources. You compete for share of voice within a set.
- **Quote**: "~6 unique citations per conversation (trending upward)... ~4 unique citations per turn on average when a turn includes any citations... The model triangulates." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/chatgpt-citation-sources
- **Provenance**: Research Lead, Feb 2026, ~700K conversation dataset

**Finding 1.5 — The citation economy is wide but unequal (Gini = 0.8)**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed)
- **Claim**: Top 10 domains capture only 12% of all citations. The Gini coefficient is 0.8 — highly unequal but no single source absolutely dominates. Any brand has a chance; few win big.
- **Quote**: "No single source dominates. But the distribution is highly unequal. A small number of domains capture disproportionate share while hundreds of thousands split the rest." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/chatgpt-citation-sources
- **Provenance**: Feb 2026 research, ~700K ChatGPT conversations

---

#### 2. Query Fanout — How Prompts Decompose into Sub-Queries

**Finding 2.1 — Single prompts fan out into multiple sub-queries**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles, proprietary data)
- **Claim**: When users ask an AI a question, the model transforms that single prompt into multiple high-intent search queries before retrieving information. These fanout queries — not the original prompt — determine which sources get cited.
- **Quote**: "When users interact with Answer Engines like Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini, they rarely see what's happening under the hood. These models often transform a single user prompt into multiple high-intent search queries to gather information and provide comprehensive answers. We call this 'query fanout.'" — https://tryprofound.com/blog/introducing-query-fanouts
- **Provenance**: Introduced Oct 2025, expanded Nov 2025, cited as foundational in all subsequent AEO content

**Finding 2.2 — Average fanout is 2.4 queries per prompt**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed from 250M AI responses analysis)
- **Claim**: A single question generates an average of 2.4 underlying queries. A prompt about cinnamon generated intent families such as "gourmet spice shops," "specialty spice shops," and "grocery stores near me."
- **Quote**: "A single question produces an average of 2.4 underlying queries." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/josh-blyskal-tech-seo-connect-deck-2025
- **Provenance**: 250M AI response analysis, Tech SEO Connect deck, Dec 2025

**Finding 2.3 — AI adds specific modifiers to fanout queries**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, proprietary scale data)
- **Claim**: The most common words ChatGPT adds to user prompts when generating fanout queries include "best," "top," "reviews," and "2025." Content that doesn't include these modifier contexts is invisible to the sub-queries AI actually uses.
- **Quote**: "Some of the most common additions include 'best,' 'top,' 'reviews,' and '2025.' Through this process, we're beginning to uncover a first look into how AI interprets and prioritizes user prompts." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/introducing-query-fanouts
- **Provenance**: Proprietary dataset from millions of daily prompts, Oct 2025

**Finding 2.4 — Topical breadth and depth is rewarded by fanout**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: Query fanout rewards sites with topical authority — if a site covers a topic comprehensively, multiple fanout sub-queries may each pull from different pages on the same site.
- **Quote**: "Google AI Mode use the query fan-out technique... This will reward sites with topical breadth and depth, the ones that feature content that covers each facet in-depth. If your site is seen as an authority on the whole topic, multiple subqueries might pull from different pages on your site." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/aeo-vs-geo
- **Provenance**: AEO vs GEO, June 2025

---

#### 3. Structural Patterns — Heading Styles, Paragraph Shape, Section Self-Containment, Answer-First Ordering

**Finding 3.1 — Answer-first structure (30–60 word direct answer at the top)**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Lead each piece with a 30–60 word direct answer to the core question, followed by 2–3 atomic paragraphs of 1–3 sentences each, then a scannable bullet list, then an example.
- **Quote**: "Start with an answer-first content structure. Lead with a 30 to 60-word direct answer to the core question, followed by 2 to 3 atomic paragraphs of 1 to 3 sentences each that provide context or detail. Add a scannable bulleted list summarizing key points, then include a brief example or use case. This pattern increases clarity and parsing speed for AI systems." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article (Jan 2026), consistent with GEO framework (July 2025)

**Finding 3.2 — Atomic paragraphs of 1–3 sentences**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Paragraphs should be 1–3 sentences — modular, self-contained units that AI systems can parse and reassemble.
- **Quote**: "Write atomic paragraphs of 1 to 3 sentences, use bullet lists for steps or definitions, and include short tables for comparisons or specifications. This modular structure supports extractability by creating discrete, self-contained units of information." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article (Jan 2026)

**Finding 3.3 — Numbered headings with statistics outperform generic headings**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: Titles and headings that include figures, percentages, or exact statistics had improved citation rates. They help AI models pinpoint precise, actionable content.
- **Quote**: "Numbered Headings: Titles that include figures, percentages, or exact statistics had improved citation rates. They help AI models pinpoint precise, actionable content." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: Profound's 2,000-page empirical website research, July 2025

**Finding 3.4 — Optimal word count: 1,500–2,500 words**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: Word counts between 1,500 and 2,500 and a balanced mix of bullet points and body text tend to perform optimally for AI citation.
- **Quote**: "Our findings suggest that word counts between 1,500 and 2,500 and a balanced mix of bullet points and body text tend to perform optimally." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research study, July 2025

**Finding 3.5 — Section self-containment (optimize each section as a standalone snippet)**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Each content section should be optimized as a standalone snippet — capable of answering a specific sub-query independently without requiring surrounding context.
- **Quote**: "AI search engines don't index or retrieve whole pages — they break content into passages or 'chunks'... That's why you should optimize each section like a standalone snippet." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/aeo-vs-geo
- **Provenance**: AEO vs GEO article, June 2025

**Finding 3.6 — Question-based headings that mirror natural language queries**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Headings should be phrased as questions that mirror how users actually ask questions conversationally, enabling AI to map content directly to relevant queries.
- **Quote**: "Structure content in Q&A format using clear, question-based headings that mirror natural language queries... When headings match the exact phrasing users employ in conversational search, AI systems can more easily map your content to relevant queries." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article, Jan 2026

**Finding 3.7 — Tables and HTML structural elements significantly boost citation rates**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: HTML tables for structured data were significantly more likely to be cited by AI. Tables allow engines to accurately parse and extract structured details.
- **Quote**: "HTML Tables: Pages utilizing tables to format data were significantly more likely to be referenced by AI. Tables allow engines to accurately parse and extract structured details, whether that's a pricing matrix, feature breakdown, or data overview." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page empirical research, July 2025

**Finding 3.8 — Detailed alt text improves AI detection**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from empirical research)
- **Claim**: Well-written, specific alt text shows strong alignment with AI detection and inclusion — it's not just an accessibility concern.
- **Quote**: "Well-written, specific alt text doesn't just boost accessibility—it also shows strong alignment with better AI detection and inclusion." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research, July 2025

---

#### 4. Schema / Structured Data — Which JSON-LD Types Matter Most

**Finding 4.1 — FAQPage schema produces "noticeable citation gains"**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: Implementing FAQ schema resulted in noticeable citation gains, confirming structured Q&A formats are strong signals for AI crawlers.
- **Quote**: "FAQ Schema Markup: Implementing FAQ schema resulted in noticeable citation gains, confirming that structured Q&A formats are strong signals for AI crawlers." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research, July 2025

**Finding 4.2 — HowTo schema produces the largest boost for procedural content**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: For instructional content, HowTo schema showed the largest boost in citation frequency of any schema type tested — though narrower in applicability.
- **Quote**: "'How To' Schema (for procedural content): For instructional material, the HowTo schema showed the largest boost in citation frequency. While it's narrower in use, the return is substantial if you're sharing guides." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research, July 2025

**Finding 4.3 — FAQ schema is favored over generic Article schema in ML model**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from AEO content score ML model documentation)
- **Claim**: The Profound AEO Content Score ML model — trained on millions of top-cited pages — explicitly identified that "certain JSON schemas (like FAQ) are favored over generic article schemas."
- **Quote**: "Structured data usage, where certain JSON schemas (like FAQ) are favored over generic article schemas." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/upgrading-content-optimization-with-the-aeo-content-score
- **Provenance**: AEO Content Score launch, Oct 2025; ML model trained on millions of cited pages

**Finding 4.4 — Schema type should match page intent**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: Match schema type to page purpose: Article for editorial content, FAQPage for Q&A sections, Product for products, HowTo for guides. Mismatched schema is worse than generic schema.
- **Quote**: "Add schema types that match your page intent, such as Article, FAQPage, Product, or HowTo. Ensure FAQ entries provide direct, concise answers rather than redirecting users to find information elsewhere." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article, Jan 2026

**Finding 4.5 — llms.txt as a structural signal for AI access**
- **Confidence**: Medium (one dedicated article, endorsed in GEO 10-step framework)
- **Claim**: llms.txt is a standardized markdown file at the site root that functions as a curated index for LLMs — directing them to high-priority, machine-readable content while filtering out noise. Profound's next-aeo npm package auto-generates it for Next.js sites.
- **Quote**: "The llms.txt file is a standardized markdown document hosted at a website's root path... Unlike traditional sitemaps or robots.txt files, llms.txt is explicitly designed to optimize LLM inference by reducing noise and surfacing high-value content." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-llms-txt-guide

#### 9. Anti-Patterns — What Profound Explicitly Says NOT to Do

**Finding 9.1 — Do not keyword-stuff**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: Keyword stuffing is explicitly dead for AI citation. Structural HTML and semantic clarity matter more than keyword density.
- **Quote**: "The days of keyword stuffing are behind us. To thrive in AI discovery, your site must deliver clean, structured, and meaningfully tagged HTML alongside your keywords." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025

**Finding 9.2 — Do not treat Markdown as a silver bullet over HTML**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, controlled experiment, n=381 pages)

### Conflicts

- **Markdown vs HTML**: Some community practitioners claim Markdown significantly boosts bot traffic. Profound's own RCT (381 pages, 3 weeks) found only a marginal directional advantage (~16% mean lift, ~1 extra median bot visit) driven entirely by high-traffic outlier pages — not statistically significant. Profound treats this as "not a priority lever, at least not yet." The conflict is with community anecdote, not with other Profound articles. Profound's position: focus on content quality over format.
- **Google ranking vs. AI citation**: Profound simultaneously says (a) 99% of AI Mode URLs appear in Google's top 20 (strong SEO foundation required) and (b) for commercial queries, ChatGPT-Google overlap can be as low as 8% with near-inverse correlation. These are reconcilable — SEO is the "qualifying round" but citation requires additional AEO-specific structure. Both are stated across multiple articles and the tension is acknowledged explicitly.

### Gaps

The following are not covered by Profound's public editorial content — you will need other sources for a complete AEO skill:

1. **Specific FAQ count norms** — Profound identifies FAQs as the single most differentiating content feature (848% more FAQs in cited products) but never specifies a target count for blog posts or articles. The 848% figure comes from e-commerce product pages, not editorial content.
2. **Voice assistant optimization specifics** — Profound mentions voice assistants (Siri, Alexa) as AEO targets repeatedly but publishes no data on voice-specific citation patterns or content formatting differences.
3. **Multimodal content (video/image) citation mechanisms** — The GEO framework mentions "embed charts, infographics, and video transcripts with descriptive captions" but notes most AI crawlers cannot parse JavaScript. No controlled data on image/video citation lift.
4. **Internal linking strategy for AEO** — Standard SEO guidance but Profound publishes nothing on how internal link architecture affects chunk retrieval or entity co-citation.
5. **Content length thresholds for specific content types** — The 1,500–2,500 word finding is from the broad empirical study. No type-specific guidance (e.g., changelog vs. blog vs. docs) is published.
6. **Structured data for non-article types** — HowTo and FAQPage are covered. Profound does not publish guidance on BreadcrumbList, SiteLinksSearchBox, SpeakableSpecification, or other schema types and their AEO relevance.
7. **Perplexity-specific optimization** — Profound identifies Perplexity as a major platform (46.7% of its top-10 citations are Reddit, it digs for older content peaking Q1 2024) but does not publish a Perplexity-specific content strategy guide.
8. **Negative brand sentiment management** — Profound notes AI cites negative and positive sentiment at nearly equal rates (~5% vs 6.1%) but publishes no guidance on suppression or reputation management in AI contexts.
9. **The specific semantic alignment scoring in their ML model** — Profound says their model evaluates "semantic alignment between the page's core content and prompts you want to optimize" but provides no method for calculating or estimating this externally.
10. **Claude-specific citation patterns** — Claude support was added to Profound's platform in August 2025, but no dedicated citation pattern research for Claude has been published as of the crawl date.

### Local Relevance

None — this is an investigation of an external methodology source, not connected to the local codebase. The skill being drafted will be a standalone reference artifact in the Lightfast monorepo.

I'm designing a durable "AEO methodology" skill for an AI coding assistant — it'll be the root best-practices reference that per-content-type skills (changelog, blog, docs) sit on top of. Profound (tryprofound.com) is the most-cited source for Answer Engine Optimization methodology because they have ML models trained on millions of top-cited pages across AI platforms (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Google AI Overviews, etc.). ## Task

## Analysis: Profound (tryprofound.com) AEO Methodology — Comprehensive Knowledge Synthesis

### Summary

Profound has published the most data-grounded public AEO methodology available, drawing on datasets ranging from 80,000 to 700,000+ real prompts across ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and other engines. Their core thesis is that AI engines retrieve at the chunk level, fan user prompts out into multiple sub-queries, and cite a handful of sources per response from a probabilistic and highly volatile pool. Content that wins citations is structurally explicit, answer-first, schema-marked, and distributed to sources AI engines already trust — with platform-specific strategies required because engine citation pools overlap by only 6–16%.

---

### Key Findings by Category

---

#### 1. Retrieval Model — How AI Engines Chunk, Retrieve, and Cite Content

**Finding 1.1 — Chunk-level retrieval, not page-level**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: AI search engines do not index or retrieve whole pages. They break content into passages and retrieve the most relevant segments for synthesis.
- **Quote**: "AI search engines don't index or retrieve whole pages — they break content into passages or 'chunks' and retrieve the most relevant segments for synthesis. That's why you should optimize each section like a standalone snippet." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/aeo-vs-geo (citing Aleyda Solis checklist, endorsed by Profound)
- **Provenance**: AEO vs GEO article (June 2025), repeated as foundational principle in AEO content score article (Oct 2025) and GEO framework (July 2025)

**Finding 1.2 — RAG is the underlying retrieval architecture**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: AI engines use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) — real-time web retrieval to augment model responses — prioritizing relevance, recency, and trust.
- **Quote**: "AI engines rely on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), the process of augmenting a generative model with external documents retrieved in real time to produce more accurate answers. This retrieval logic prioritizes relevance, recency, and trust. This means that ChatGPT now does web search for most questions instead of relying on its existing training data." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/generative-engine-optimization-geo-guide-2025
- **Provenance**: GEO Framework (July 2025)

**Finding 1.3 — Turn 1 is the critical citation moment**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed from ~700K conversation sample)
- **Claim**: Turn 1 of a conversation is 2.5x more likely to trigger citations than Turn 10, and nearly 4x more likely than Turn 20. Opening questions trigger web searches; follow-ups rarely do.
- **Quote**: "Turn 1 is everything. Users' opening questions trigger web searches; follow-ups rarely do. Turn 1 is 2.5× more likely to trigger citations than turn 10, and nearly 4× more likely than turn 20. If you want to be cited, you need to win the 'first question.'" — https://tryprofound.com/blog/chatgpt-citation-sources
- **Provenance**: Original research on ~730,000 ChatGPT conversations, Oct–Dec 2025

**Finding 1.4 — AI cites ~4–6 sources per turn, not one winner**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed)
- **Claim**: When ChatGPT cites, it cites ~4 unique domains per turn and ~6 per conversation. 66% of cited turns have 1–4 unique sources. You compete for share of voice within a set.
- **Quote**: "~6 unique citations per conversation (trending upward)... ~4 unique citations per turn on average when a turn includes any citations... The model triangulates." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/chatgpt-citation-sources
- **Provenance**: Research Lead, Feb 2026, ~700K conversation dataset

**Finding 1.5 — The citation economy is wide but unequal (Gini = 0.8)**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed)
- **Claim**: Top 10 domains capture only 12% of all citations. The Gini coefficient is 0.8 — highly unequal but no single source absolutely dominates. Any brand has a chance; few win big.
- **Quote**: "No single source dominates. But the distribution is highly unequal. A small number of domains capture disproportionate share while hundreds of thousands split the rest." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/chatgpt-citation-sources
- **Provenance**: Feb 2026 research, ~700K ChatGPT conversations

---

#### 2. Query Fanout — How Prompts Decompose into Sub-Queries

**Finding 2.1 — Single prompts fan out into multiple sub-queries**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles, proprietary data)
- **Claim**: When users ask an AI a question, the model transforms that single prompt into multiple high-intent search queries before retrieving information. These fanout queries — not the original prompt — determine which sources get cited.
- **Quote**: "When users interact with Answer Engines like Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini, they rarely see what's happening under the hood. These models often transform a single user prompt into multiple high-intent search queries to gather information and provide comprehensive answers. We call this 'query fanout.'" — https://tryprofound.com/blog/introducing-query-fanouts
- **Provenance**: Introduced Oct 2025, expanded Nov 2025, cited as foundational in all subsequent AEO content

**Finding 2.2 — Average fanout is 2.4 queries per prompt**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed from 250M AI responses analysis)
- **Claim**: A single question generates an average of 2.4 underlying queries. A prompt about cinnamon generated intent families such as "gourmet spice shops," "specialty spice shops," and "grocery stores near me."
- **Quote**: "A single question produces an average of 2.4 underlying queries." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/josh-blyskal-tech-seo-connect-deck-2025
- **Provenance**: 250M AI response analysis, Tech SEO Connect deck, Dec 2025

**Finding 2.3 — AI adds specific modifiers to fanout queries**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, proprietary scale data)
- **Claim**: The most common words ChatGPT adds to user prompts when generating fanout queries include "best," "top," "reviews," and "2025." Content that doesn't include these modifier contexts is invisible to the sub-queries AI actually uses.
- **Quote**: "Some of the most common additions include 'best,' 'top,' 'reviews,' and '2025.' Through this process, we're beginning to uncover a first look into how AI interprets and prioritizes user prompts." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/introducing-query-fanouts
- **Provenance**: Proprietary dataset from millions of daily prompts, Oct 2025

**Finding 2.4 — Topical breadth and depth is rewarded by fanout**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: Query fanout rewards sites with topical authority — if a site covers a topic comprehensively, multiple fanout sub-queries may each pull from different pages on the same site.
- **Quote**: "Google AI Mode use the query fan-out technique... This will reward sites with topical breadth and depth, the ones that feature content that covers each facet in-depth. If your site is seen as an authority on the whole topic, multiple subqueries might pull from different pages on your site." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/aeo-vs-geo
- **Provenance**: AEO vs GEO, June 2025

---

#### 3. Structural Patterns — Heading Styles, Paragraph Shape, Section Self-Containment, Answer-First Ordering

**Finding 3.1 — Answer-first structure (30–60 word direct answer at the top)**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Lead each piece with a 30–60 word direct answer to the core question, followed by 2–3 atomic paragraphs of 1–3 sentences each, then a scannable bullet list, then an example.
- **Quote**: "Start with an answer-first content structure. Lead with a 30 to 60-word direct answer to the core question, followed by 2 to 3 atomic paragraphs of 1 to 3 sentences each that provide context or detail. Add a scannable bulleted list summarizing key points, then include a brief example or use case. This pattern increases clarity and parsing speed for AI systems." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article (Jan 2026), consistent with GEO framework (July 2025)

**Finding 3.2 — Atomic paragraphs of 1–3 sentences**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Paragraphs should be 1–3 sentences — modular, self-contained units that AI systems can parse and reassemble.
- **Quote**: "Write atomic paragraphs of 1 to 3 sentences, use bullet lists for steps or definitions, and include short tables for comparisons or specifications. This modular structure supports extractability by creating discrete, self-contained units of information." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article (Jan 2026)

**Finding 3.3 — Numbered headings with statistics outperform generic headings**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: Titles and headings that include figures, percentages, or exact statistics had improved citation rates. They help AI models pinpoint precise, actionable content.
- **Quote**: "Numbered Headings: Titles that include figures, percentages, or exact statistics had improved citation rates. They help AI models pinpoint precise, actionable content." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: Profound's 2,000-page empirical website research, July 2025

**Finding 3.4 — Optimal word count: 1,500–2,500 words**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: Word counts between 1,500 and 2,500 and a balanced mix of bullet points and body text tend to perform optimally for AI citation.
- **Quote**: "Our findings suggest that word counts between 1,500 and 2,500 and a balanced mix of bullet points and body text tend to perform optimally." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research study, July 2025

**Finding 3.5 — Section self-containment (optimize each section as a standalone snippet)**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Each content section should be optimized as a standalone snippet — capable of answering a specific sub-query independently without requiring surrounding context.
- **Quote**: "AI search engines don't index or retrieve whole pages — they break content into passages or 'chunks'... That's why you should optimize each section like a standalone snippet." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/aeo-vs-geo
- **Provenance**: AEO vs GEO article, June 2025

**Finding 3.6 — Question-based headings that mirror natural language queries**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, multiple articles)
- **Claim**: Headings should be phrased as questions that mirror how users actually ask questions conversationally, enabling AI to map content directly to relevant queries.
- **Quote**: "Structure content in Q&A format using clear, question-based headings that mirror natural language queries... When headings match the exact phrasing users employ in conversational search, AI systems can more easily map your content to relevant queries." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article, Jan 2026

**Finding 3.7 — Tables and HTML structural elements significantly boost citation rates**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: HTML tables for structured data were significantly more likely to be cited by AI. Tables allow engines to accurately parse and extract structured details.
- **Quote**: "HTML Tables: Pages utilizing tables to format data were significantly more likely to be referenced by AI. Tables allow engines to accurately parse and extract structured details, whether that's a pricing matrix, feature breakdown, or data overview." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page empirical research, July 2025

**Finding 3.8 — Detailed alt text improves AI detection**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from empirical research)
- **Claim**: Well-written, specific alt text shows strong alignment with AI detection and inclusion — it's not just an accessibility concern.
- **Quote**: "Well-written, specific alt text doesn't just boost accessibility—it also shows strong alignment with better AI detection and inclusion." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research, July 2025

#### 4. Schema / Structured Data — Which JSON-LD Types Matter Most

**Finding 4.1 — FAQPage schema produces "noticeable citation gains"**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: Implementing FAQ schema resulted in noticeable citation gains, confirming structured Q&A formats are strong signals for AI crawlers.
- **Quote**: "FAQ Schema Markup: Implementing FAQ schema resulted in noticeable citation gains, confirming that structured Q&A formats are strong signals for AI crawlers." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research, July 2025

**Finding 4.2 — HowTo schema produces the largest boost for procedural content**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from 2,000+ page empirical research)
- **Claim**: For instructional content, HowTo schema showed the largest boost in citation frequency of any schema type tested — though narrower in applicability.
- **Quote**: "'How To' Schema (for procedural content): For instructional material, the HowTo schema showed the largest boost in citation frequency. While it's narrower in use, the return is substantial if you're sharing guides." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research, July 2025

**Finding 4.3 — FAQ schema is favored over generic Article schema in ML model**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from AEO content score ML model documentation)
- **Claim**: The Profound AEO Content Score ML model — trained on millions of top-cited pages — explicitly identified that "certain JSON schemas (like FAQ) are favored over generic article schemas."
- **Quote**: "Structured data usage, where certain JSON schemas (like FAQ) are favored over generic article schemas." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/upgrading-content-optimization-with-the-aeo-content-score

**Finding 4.4 — Schema type should match page intent**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: Match schema type to page purpose: Article for editorial content, FAQPage for Q&A sections, Product for products, HowTo for guides. Mismatched schema is worse than generic schema.
- **Quote**: "Add schema types that match your page intent, such as Article, FAQPage, Product, or HowTo. Ensure FAQ entries provide direct, concise answers rather than redirecting users to find information elsewhere." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-answer-engine-optimization
- **Provenance**: What is AEO article, Jan 2026

**Finding 4.5 — llms.txt as a structural signal for AI access**
- **Confidence**: Medium (one dedicated article, endorsed in GEO 10-step framework)
- **Claim**: llms.txt is a standardized markdown file at the site root that functions as a curated index for LLMs — directing them to high-priority, machine-readable content while filtering out noise. Profound's next-aeo npm package auto-generates it for Next.js sites.
- **Quote**: "The llms.txt file is a standardized markdown document hosted at a website's root path... Unlike traditional sitemaps or robots.txt files, llms.txt is explicitly designed to optimize LLM inference by reducing noise and surfacing high-value content." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/what-is-llms-txt-guide

- **Claim**: FAQ entries must contain the actual answer inline — not "learn more" or "see our guide." AI cannot follow redirects inside schema.

- **Quote**: "Include Expert Commentary & Distinctive Insights: Generative AI will value unique perspectives. If your company has subject-matter experts, feature their commentary prominently. Highlight brand values, insider tips, proprietary research, and distinct attributes." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/answer-engine-optimization-aeo-guide-for-marketers-2025
- **Provenance**: AEO Marketer's Playbook, Dec 2025

---

#### 9. Anti-Patterns — What Profound Explicitly Says NOT to Do

**Finding 9.1 — Do not keyword-stuff**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: Keyword stuffing is explicitly dead for AI citation. Structural HTML and semantic clarity matter more than keyword density.
- **Quote**: "The days of keyword stuffing are behind us. To thrive in AI discovery, your site must deliver clean, structured, and meaningfully tagged HTML alongside your keywords." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/how-to-optimize-answer-engines-2025
- **Provenance**: 2,000-page research article, July 2025

**Finding 9.2 — Do not treat Markdown as a silver bullet over HTML**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, controlled experiment, n=381 pages)
- **Claim**: The theory that serving Markdown to AI crawlers produces dramatically more bot traffic is not supported. A controlled RCT on 381 pages showed only ~1 extra median visit over 3 weeks — not statistically significant. LLMs are already optimized for HTML parsing.
- **Quote**: "TL;DR: We A/B tested 381 pages across six sites. Markdown pages saw ~1 extra median bot visit over three weeks. Not nothing, but not the game-changer some have hypothesized." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/does-markdown-increase-ai-bot-traffic
- **Provenance**: Randomized controlled experiment, Jan–Feb 2026

**Finding 9.3 — Do not block AI crawlers in robots.txt**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: Blocking OAI-SearchBot, GPTBot, ChatGPT-User, or OAI-Operator in robots.txt removes your content from AI shopping and citation results entirely.
- **Quote**: "If this crawler is blocked in your robots.txt, your content won't be visible in product results — regardless of quality." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/get-your-product-discovered-in-chatgpt-shopping
- **Provenance**: ChatGPT product discovery article, July 2025

**Finding 9.4 — Do not use a one-size-fits-all platform strategy**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, supported by citation overlap data)
- **Claim**: ~89% of AI citations come from completely different sources depending on which model is queried. A strategy optimized for one engine is not optimized for another. Platform-specific strategies are required.
- **Quote**: "Nearly 89% of AI citations come from completely different sources depending on which model users query... The era of one-size-fits-all content strategy is over." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/citation-overlap-strategy
- **Provenance**: 100K prompt citation overlap study, July 2025

**Finding 9.5 — Do not use sales-y or marketing jargon language**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, from Reddit/AI data)
- **Claim**: AI filters out content that "feels sales-y or like marketing jargon." Authentic, balanced, helpful language is what gets cited — AI cites both positive and negative brand sentiment at nearly equal rates (~5% vs 6.1%), proving it seeks genuine evaluation.
- **Quote**: "Our data shows citation rates for positive (5%) and negative (6.1%) brand sentiment are nearly identical. This proves the AI is seeking authentic evaluation, not just praise." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/the-data-on-reddit-and-ai-search
- **Provenance**: Reddit x Profound, Nov 2025, 4B+ citations

**Finding 9.6 — Do not optimize for clicks as the primary metric**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit)
- **Claim**: The "click" as the unit of measurement is becoming obsolete. 37.5% of ChatGPT interactions are generative intent (create/draft/do), not informational intent — meaning traditional traffic-based KPIs are fundamentally broken for AI search.
- **Quote**: "Most companies are still optimizing for discoverability when they should be optimizing for recommendability, still chasing rankings when they should be chasing citations, and still measuring clicks when they should be measuring influence." — https://tryprofound.com/blog/chatgpt-intent-landmark-study
- **Provenance**: 50M+ ChatGPT prompt intent study, June 2025

**Finding 9.7 — Do not assume Google ranking = AI citation**
- **Confidence**: Strong (explicit, data-backed)
- **Claim**: For commercial/product queries, ChatGPT and Google results have as little as 8% URL overlap, sometimes with a near-inverse correlation (r = –0.98 for "best running shoes"). Dominating Google does not mean you appear in ChatGPT.
- **Quote**: "Overlap: Only ~8% of URLs matched between ChatGPT and Google... Negative Correlation (r ≈ –0.98): A shocking –0.98 correlation, meaning the more ChatGPT liked a URL, the less likely Google was to rank it." — https://tryprofound.com/resources/articles/the-surprising-gap-between-chatgpt-and-google
- **Provenance**: 650+ ChatGPT queries / 200 Google SERP pulls, Feb 2025

Sentiment Score: How often an AI system gives a positive or negative review of your product. ---
name: lightfast-aeo
description: |
  Root Answer Engine Optimization (AEO) methodology for any Lightfast content — changelog, blog,
  docs, integrations, legal. Use when authoring or reviewing content destined for AI engine
  retrieval (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Google AI Overviews). Per-content-type skills
  (lightfast-changelog, lightfast-blog, etc.) compose this skill for editorial voice and
  structural hierarchy. Triggers on requests to write, draft, review, or rewrite any
  Lightfast-published content.
---

# Lightfast AEO Methodology

This skill encodes the durable principles for writing content that AI engines retrieve and cite. It is content-type agnostic. Per-type skills layer voice, audience, and section ordering on top.

Methodology grounded in Profound's published research (tryprofound.com), which trains its AEO scoring model on millions of top-cited pages across answer engines.

## The retrieval model

AI engines retrieve at the **chunk level**, not the page level. They break a page into passages and surface the most relevant passage for a sub-query. The unit of optimization is the H2 section, not the article.

A user's prompt fans out into **~2.4 sub-queries** on average before retrieval. The engine commonly adds modifiers like *best*, *top*, *reviews*, and the current year. Headings that mirror those decomposed sub-queries get retrieved; headings that match the original prompt often do not.

Engines cite **~4 unique sources per turn** and **~6 per conversation**. You compete for share-of-voice within a citation set, not for a single winner slot.

## Structural principles

### Answer-first ordering

Each section leads with a 30–60 word direct answer to the heading question, then 2–3 atomic paragraphs of context, then a scannable list, then an example. AI extraction reads top-down and stops early — the answer must be in the first block.

Do not open with filler ("Here's what's new", "In this post we'll cover"). That position is the most valuable retrieval real estate on the page.

### Question-based H2 headings

Every H2 must be phrased as a question that mirrors a fanout sub-query.

| Avoid | Prefer |
|---|---|
| `## Neural Pipeline` | `## How does the neural pipeline process events?` |
| `## New Features` | `## What's new in this release?` |
| `## Performance` | `## What performance improvements ship in this release?` |
| `## API` | `## How do I access this programmatically?` |

Headings that include numbers, percentages, or specific statistics get cited more often than generic phrasings — they help AI pinpoint precise content.

### Self-contained sections

A reader (human or model) must be able to extract any H2 section in isolation and have it make sense. No dangling pronouns referring to earlier sections. No "as mentioned above". The product name and key entities should appear within the section, not relied upon from the intro.

### Atomic paragraphs

1–3 sentences per paragraph. One idea per paragraph. No paragraphs that depend on the prior paragraph for context. This is the unit AI engines reassemble.

### Tables for structured data

HTML tables for comparisons, specs, pricing, feature matrices, and parameter references get cited at materially higher rates than the same data expressed as prose. Reach for a table whenever data is comparative or grid-shaped.

### Detailed alt text

Alt text is an AI signal, not just an accessibility requirement. Describe what's in the image specifically (`"Triage runtime dashboard showing 12 active workflows and 3 queued events"`) — not generically (`"dashboard screenshot"`).

### Length

Target roughly **1,500–2,500 words** per long-form piece, with a balanced mix of prose and bullets. Per-type skills can override (a release note can be shorter, a deep technical post can be longer).

## Trust block

Render a trust block near the top of every page containing:

- Author name + credentials (jobTitle in author markup)
- Published date and last-updated date
- 1–2 cited external sources where applicable
- 40–50 word answer summary (the TL;DR)

This block doubles as the most-extractable answer block and as the model-readable proof of provenance. Update the last-updated date whenever content changes — recency is an explicit ranking signal in AEO scoring.

## FAQ construction

FAQ sections become FAQPage JSON-LD, the highest-impact structured data for citation according to Profound's empirical research. Treat the FAQ as a primary citation surface, not an afterthought.

### Rules

- **Direct answers only** — never "see our docs" or "learn more". The model cannot follow links from inside schema.
- **1–3 sentences per answer** — concise enough to be lifted whole into a model response.
- **Self-contained** — each Q&A pair must make sense without the others.
- **Conversational, not sales-y** — AI explicitly filters out marketing language. Write the way a senior engineer would answer a teammate.
- **Include the product name** — the entity needs to appear in the answer for brand recognition in cited responses.

### Categories to cover

For any non-trivial piece, ensure the FAQ spans:

1. **Discovery** — "What is X?", "What does X do?"
2. **Mechanism** — "How does X work?"
3. **Capability** — "Can I do Y with X?", "Does X support Z?"
4. **Specifics** — "What integrations / sources / parameters does X handle?"

Per-type skills set the count target.

## Format selection

| Format | Use when |
|---|---|
| **Comparative** ("X vs Y", "Best X for Y") | The piece evaluates options or alternatives — these are among the highest-cited formats |
| **HowTo** schema | The piece is procedural / step-by-step — produces the largest single citation lift of any schema type |
| **FAQPage** schema | Any piece with a Q&A section — the highest-cited schema for editorial content |
| **Article / BlogPosting** | Default for narrative content |
| **TL;DR block** | Always — combined with the trust block |

Schema type must match page intent. Mismatched schema (Article on a HowTo, generic Article when FAQ is present) underperforms generic markup.

## Anti-patterns

- **Keyword stuffing** — explicitly dead. Structural HTML and semantic clarity outweigh density.
- **Sales-y / marketing jargon** — AI filters it out. Authentic, balanced language gets cited; promotional language does not.
- **Redirect-style FAQ answers** — "see our guide" / "click here" — the model cannot follow.
- **Generic headings** — `## Features`, `## Overview` — they don't match any fanout sub-query.
- **Long paragraphs** — anything over 3 sentences is a chunk that won't extract cleanly.
- **Combining multiple features under one H2** — each feature is a separate retrievable chunk; give each its own H2.
- **Filler openings** — wastes the highest-value retrieval position.
- **Skipping the trust block** — the model has no provenance signal without it.

## Validation

Frontmatter shape and constraints are enforced by Zod schemas in the codebase. Do not encode field rules in this skill — they will rot.

Workflow:

1. If authoring from scratch, locate the relevant content schema by grepping the codebase (e.g., `ChangelogEntrySchema`, `BlogPostSchema`).
2. Draft frontmatter and body.
3. Run typecheck — it surfaces any constraint violations with the current schema rules.

```bash
pnpm --filter @lightfast/www typecheck
```

Only the methodology in this skill is durable. Field names, character limits, and enum values live in the codebase.

## Sources

- tryprofound.com/blog — primary research and AEO methodology
- tryprofound.com/resources/articles — editorial guides
- Profound's AEO Content Score model (Oct 2025) — ML signals for chunk extractability, schema usage, recency, semantic alignment
- Profound query fanout research (Oct–Nov 2025) — sub-query decomposition data
- Profound 2,000-page empirical study (July 2025) — schema lift, table impact, word count, alt text findings

- Published date and last-updated date
- 1–2 cited external sources where applicable
- 40–50 word answer summary (the TL;DR)

This block doubles as the most-extractable answer block and as the model-readable proof of provenance. Update the last-updated date whenever content changes — recency is an explicit ranking signal in AEO scoring.

## FAQ construction

FAQ sections become FAQPage JSON-LD, the highest-impact structured data for citation according to Profound's empirical research. Treat the FAQ as a primary citation surface, not an afterthought.

### Rules

- **Direct answers only** — never "see our docs" or "learn more". The model cannot follow links from inside schema.
- **1–3 sentences per answer** — concise enough to be lifted whole into a model response.
- **Self-contained** — each Q&A pair must make sense without the others.
- **Conversational, not sales-y** — AI explicitly filters out marketing language. Write the way a senior engineer would answer a teammate.
- **Include the product name** — the entity needs to appear in the answer for brand recognition in cited responses.

### Categories to cover

For any non-trivial piece, ensure the FAQ spans:

1. **Discovery** — "What is X?", "What does X do?"
2. **Mechanism** — "How does X work?"
3. **Capability** — "Can I do Y with X?", "Does X support Z?"
4. **Specifics** — "What integrations / sources / parameters does X handle?"

Per-type skills set the count target.

## Format selection

| Format | Use when |
|---|---|
| **Comparative** ("X vs Y", "Best X for Y") | The piece evaluates options or alternatives — these are among the highest-cited formats |
| **HowTo** schema | The piece is procedural / step-by-step — produces the largest single citation lift of any schema type |
| **FAQPage** schema | Any piece with a Q&A section — the highest-cited schema for editorial content |
| **Article / BlogPosting** | Default for narrative content |
| **TL;DR block** | Always — combined with the trust block |

Schema type must match page intent. Mismatched schema (Article on a HowTo, generic Article when FAQ is present) underperforms generic markup.

## Anti-patterns

- **Keyword stuffing** — explicitly dead. Structural HTML and semantic clarity outweigh density.
- **Sales-y / marketing jargon** — AI filters it out. Authentic, balanced language gets cited; promotional language does not.
- **Redirect-style FAQ answers** — "see our guide" / "click here" — the model cannot follow.
- **Generic headings** — `## Features`, `## Overview` — they don't match any fanout sub-query.
- **Long paragraphs** — anything over 3 sentences is a chunk that won't extract cleanly.
- **Combining multiple features under one H2** — each feature is a separate retrievable chunk; give each its own H2.
- **Filler openings** — wastes the highest-value retrieval position.
- **Skipping the trust block** — the model has no provenance signal without it.

## Validation

Frontmatter shape and constraints are enforced by Zod schemas in the codebase. Do not encode field rules in this skill — they will rot.

Workflow:

1. If authoring from scratch, locate the relevant content schema by grepping the codebase (e.g., `ChangelogEntrySchema`, `BlogPostSchema`).
2. Draft frontmatter and body.
3. Run typecheck — it surfaces any constraint violations with the current schema rules.

```bash
pnpm --filter @lightfast/www typecheck
```

Only the methodology in this skill is durable. Field names, character limits, and enum values live in the codebase.

## Sources

- tryprofound.com/blog — primary research and AEO methodology
- tryprofound.com/resources/articles — editorial guides
- Profound's AEO Content Score model (Oct 2025) — ML signals for chunk extractability, schema usage, recency, semantic alignment
- Profound query fanout research (Oct–Nov 2025) — sub-query decomposition data
- Profound 2,000-page empirical study (July 2025) — schema lift, table impact, word count, alt text findings

description: |

  reviewing, or rewriting content in apps/www/src/content/changelog/. Composes the lightfast-aeo
  skill for retrieval methodology — this skill only encodes what is changelog-specific (tone,
  audience, section ordering, length).
